coredata.dc:title: Enhanced intracellular calcium detection using dopamine-modified graphene quantum dots with dual emission mechanisms
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Rumei
Li, Zhangliang
Chang, Pingjun
Shan, Suyan
Jiang, Xiaohui
Hu, Zhixuan
Zhang, Bei
Zhao, Yune
Ou, Shengju
coredata.dc:description: The tracking of calcium ions (Ca2+) is of great significance in clinical medicine. Dopamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (DA-GQDs) have been developed as a novel fluorescence sensor for detecting and imaging Ca2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. Upon the addition of various concentrations of Ca2+, the fluorescence intensity of DA-GQDs notably enhanced and exhibited a redshift. This behavior was regulated by the photoinduced electron transfer and internal charge transfer mechanisms, as elucidated by fluorescence titration and density functional theory calculations. The coordination of Ca2+ with the oxygen groups on DA-GQDs enhanced its sensitivity and selectivity. The detection limit was determined to be 0.05 µM, with a robust linear response range of 4.93–10.61 µM. Even in the presence of chelating agents such as gluconate, the DA-GQDs accurately quantified Ca2+. Competition experiments demonstrated that the DA-GQDs exhibited a high fluorescence response specifically for Ca2+, with negligible selectivity toward other ions and biomolecules. The CCK8 assay confirmed that the DA-GQDs are nontoxic and biocompatible. Moreover, DA-GQDs have been successfully employed for imaging intracellular Ca2+. This study provides new insights into the design of Ca2+ fluorescence sensors using biocompatible molecular components.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Dopamine
Calcium(II)
Dual fluorescent mechanism
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity of the DA-GQDs to Ca2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405115, 82371042), the Wenzhou Bureau of Science and Technology (Y20210081), Project of State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Vision Science of Wenzhou Medical University (J02-20210202), \u201CPioneer\u201D and \u201CLeading Goose\u201D R&D Program of Zhejiang (2022C03070), the Provincial Construction Project of Zhejiang (KJ-ZJ-2135), and the Huadong Medicine Joint Funds of the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LHDMY23H070004) are acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Raman spectra of (i) GQDs and (ii) DA-GQDs; (b) XRD patterns of (i) GQDs, (ii) DA-GQDs and (iii) simulated; (c) FTIR spectra of (i) DA, (ii) GQDs and (iii) DA-GQDs; (d) UV–vis spectra of (i) DA, (ii) GQDs and (iii) DA-GQDs.
TEM images of (a) GQDs and (b) the DA-GQDs; (c) AFM images of the DA-GQDs; (d) The emission spectra of (i) GQDs and (ii) DA-GQDs.
MALDI-TOF mass spectra of the (a) GQDs and (b) DA-GQDs in the 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid solution. Proposed molecular configurations of the (c) GQDs and (d) DA-GQDs.

(a) Fluorescent emission spectra of the DA-GQD fluorescence sensor in the presence of different concentrations of Ca2+. (b) and (c) Linear calibration curves for Ca2+ detection at E338nm and at E440nm, respectively. (d) Fluorescence ratio detection of Ca2+.
Surface structure of the DA-GQDs combined with Ca2+ (C, gray; H, white; O, red; N, blue; Ca, green; Cl, olive).

Selectivity of the DA-GQDs toward Ca2+ in an aqueous solution in the presence of a five-fold concentration of various ions. 1: Ca2+; 2: Fe3+; 3: Mg2+; 4: K+; 5: Sr2+; 6: Ba2+; 7: Mn2+; 8: Cr3+; 9: Ni2+; 10: Al3+; 11: Cu2+; 12: Ag+; 13: Na+; 14: glucose; 15: fructose; 16: maltose; 17: lactose; 18: glucosamine; 19: glutamate sodium (black column: DA-GQDs + interferent; Red column: DA-GQDs + interferent + Ca2+).
Confocal fluorescence images of the ARPE-19 cells incubated with the DA-GQDs and subsequently exposed to (i) 0-μM, (ii) 2.5-μM, (iii) 5.0-μM, and (iv) 7.5-μM Ca2+.(Scale: 20 μm).
Illustration of the DA-GQDs sensing Ca2+ based on ICT-PET interplaying mechanism.

head.ce:title: Enhanced intracellular calcium detection using dopamine-modified graphene quantum dots with dual emission mechanisms

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Eye Hospital, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325027, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Eye Hospital, School of Ophthalmology & Optometry, State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Visual Science, Wenzhou Medical University, Wenzhou 325027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hangzhou Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd., Zhejiang University National Park, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hangzhou Femtosecond Test Co. Ltd., Zhejiang University National Park, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The tracking of calcium ions (Ca2+) is of great significance in clinical medicine. Dopamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (DA-GQDs) have been developed as a novel fluorescence sensor for detecting and imaging Ca2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. Upon the addition of various concentrations of Ca2+, the fluorescence intensity of DA-GQDs notably enhanced and exhibited a redshift. This behavior was regulated by the photoinduced electron transfer and internal charge transfer mechanisms, as elucidated by fluorescence titration and density functional theory calculations. The coordination of Ca2+ with the oxygen groups on DA-GQDs enhanced its sensitivity and selectivity. The detection limit was determined to be 0.05 µM, with a robust linear response range of 4.93–10.61 µM. Even in the presence of chelating agents such as gluconate, the DA-GQDs accurately quantified Ca2+. Competition experiments demonstrated that the DA-GQDs exhibited a high fluorescence response specifically for Ca2+, with negligible selectivity toward other ions and biomolecules. The CCK8 assay confirmed that the DA-GQDs are nontoxic and biocompatible. Moreover, DA-GQDs have been successfully employed for imaging intracellular Ca2+. This study provides new insights into the design of Ca2+ fluorescence sensors using biocompatible molecular components.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The real-time tracing of calcium ions (Ca2+) in living cells is crucial for understanding their role in various physiological and biochemical processes. As a versatile intracellular signaling messenger, Ca2+ performs vital functions in many biological activities [1,2]. It is involved in numerous cellular processes, including cell proliferation, differentiation, and survival/death [3,4]. The occurrence and progression of several diseases such as osteoporosis, poor clotting blood function, Parkinson’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease, and Huntington’s disease are often associated with abnormal concentrations of Ca2+ in vivo [5,6]. Furthermore, increasing evidence suggests that different calcium statuses in cancer cells are related to a series of disease signals, such as tumorigenesis, angiogenesis, progression, and metastasis [7,8]. Therefore, tracking of Ca2+ in body fluids, especially intracellular Ca2+, is of paramount importance.

Traditional methods for detecting Ca2+ include colorimetric methods, atomic absorption spectroscopy, and ion-selective electrode techniques [9–12]. However, these analytical techniques require expensive equipment, sophisticated manipulations, specific sample pretreatments, and professional operators. Moreover, they are not suitable for the real-time detection of intracellular Ca2+. In contrast, fluorescent assays have attracted growing interest and have been widely applied in clinical diagnostics and biochemical analyses owing to their simplicity, high speed, good selectivity, and visualization [13,14]. Considerable efforts have been devoted to developing fluorescence sensors for monitoring of Ca2+ in vivo. The use of synthetic Ca2+ organic dyes such as MaPCa, GCaMP, and OCaMBI110 has somewhat advanced the in situ detection of Ca2+ [15–17]. However, these probes still suffer from poor water solubility, complex preparation processes, and potential leakage, greatly limiting their applicability [18]. Thus, there is an urgent need to develop a fluorescence sensor with excellent solubility, good biocompatibility, and high permeability to cells [19].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials consisting of single or multiple layers of graphene with a size range of 3–20 nm. They exhibit low cytotoxicity, excellent solubility, stable photoluminescence, and enhanced permeability and retention effect. These properties make GQDs highly applicable in sensors, biological imaging, drug delivery, and photodynamic therapy [20,21]. Currently, several studies have focused on fluorescence sensors based on GQDs for the detection of peptides, ions, pH levels, small molecules, humidity, and other biomolecules [22,23]. Efforts have also been made to design Ca2+ fluorescent sensors using carbon-based nanomaterials [24,25]; however, the understanding of the molecular mechanisms for selective Ca2+ binding to these nanosensors remains limited [26]. To date, GQD-based fluorescence sensors for detecting Ca2+, especially for imaging intracellular calcium, have been rarely reported [27]. The GQDs typically function as an electron donor-π-electron acceptor (D-π-A) system, which is advantageous for constructing an internal charge transfer (ICT)-based nanosensor [28]. By combining it with another fluorophore, a dual fluorescent nanosensor can be constructed. Such a strategy often provides high sensitivity and selectivity for the target analyte [29]. Dopamine (DA), a neurotransmitter and hormone from the phenethylamine and catecholamine families, plays a crucial role in the central nervous system and brain [30]. It is capable of donating electrons, making it an ideal candidate for strong interaction with metal ions, thereby altering the fluorescence signal [31].

Herein, we propose incorporating DA with GQDs to construct a turn-on fluorescent (DA-GQD) sensor for detecting Ca2+. The integration of GQDs with DA for constructing the sensor serves the dual purpose of enhancing detection capabilities while addressing the needs of real-time monitoring in biological system. The use of DA as an electron donor in conjunction with GQDs changes the dipole of GQDs, enhancing the sensor's selectivity and sensitivity towards Ca2+. As depicted in Scheme 1 , DA-GQDs exhibit weak fluorescence, which considerably enhances upon the addition of Ca2+ due to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process. The chelation of Ca2+ to DA-GQDs blocks the PET process and strengthens the ICT effect. Confocal microscopy images confirm that DA-GQDs can detect intracellular Ca2+ and image calcium fluxes. The cytotoxicity assay indicates that DA-GQDs are biocompatible and exhibit low toxicity, demonstrating their potential for the early detection of several clinical diseases.

Natural graphite powder was purchased from Qingdao Reagents Company (Qingdao, China). Sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), strontium chloride (SrCl2), barium chloride (BaCl2), and various sugars, including glucose, fructose, and maltose, along with dopamine, were obtained from Aldrich-Sigma. Millipore water was used throughout the experiments. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were taken using a PHILIPS EM400ST microscope at an accelerating voltage of 150 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were conducted using Cu-Kα radiation at 35 kV on a Siemens diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on an ESCALAB MK-II spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a PE Spectrum One spectrometer using KBr pellets. UV–vis spectra were obtained using a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 spectrometer. Raman spectra were documented using a Renishaw 2000 equipped with an Ar+ laser (excitation line of 514.5 nm) and an air-cooled charge-coupled device as the detector. Fluorescence emission spectra were obtained using a Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer. MALDI-TOF mass spectra were recorded by the Autoflex mass spectrometer using delayed extraction and a nitrogen laser. The ESI-Q-TOF mass spectrum was measured employing the Waters XEVO G2-XS Q-TOF spectrometer equipped with a Spray ESI source.

GQDs were synthesized according to previously reported literature [32]. In a typical experiment, an aqueous solution of GQDs (1 mg/mL) was placed into a three-neck flask. A freshly prepared mixture of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC) (1 mM) and N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (Sulfo-NHS) (1 mM) was allowed to stand for 10 min and then added to the GQDs solution at room temperature with continuous stirring. After 30 min, dopamine was gradually dripped into the above mixture to react for 24 h under stirring. The DA-GQDs were obtained by conducting dialysis in a dialysis bag (cut-off molecular weight: 1000 Da) for 3 days.

Upon the addition of various concentrations of Ca2+ ions into the DA-GQD solution, titration experiments were conducted. The mixture was incubated for 5 min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 258 nm. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
LOD = 3 σ / s l o p e
where σ is the standard deviation.

The interactions between the DA-GQDs and Ca2+ were analyzed using the DMol3 package within Materials Studio, in conjunction with density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The exchange–correlation interactions were treated with the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof generalized gradient approximation (GGA) [33]. The wave function of the valence electrons was based on the DNP basis set, analogous to the 6–31 G** basis set [34]. The convergence criterion for the self-consistent field calculations was set to 1 × 10−6 Ha. The molecular formula of GQDs was speculated to be C66H40O12, which contained numerous hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups. The GQDs were combined with DA to prepare the DA-GQDs, which are electrically neutral molecules. The adsorption energy (Ead) was calculated to estimate the binding energy between the DA-GQDs and Ca2+ using the following equation:
(2)
E ad
=
E
D A - GQDs
-
E GQDs
-
E DA
where EDA-GQDs, EGQDs, and EDA are the energies of the DA-GQD complex, GQDs, and dopamine, respectively.

The human retinal pigment epithelium cells (ARPE-19) were cultured following the method previously reported by our group [35]. Cytotoxicity was assessed using the CCK-8 assay under various concentrations and exposure durations.

ARPE-19 cells were cultured in 24-well plates for 24 h, forming two groups. The first group of cells was incubated solely with the DA-GQDs, serving as the control group. The second group was initially incubated with L-glutamate (1 mM, 20 µL) for 10 s, followed by treatment with the DA-GQDs for another 2.0 h. Fluorescence images of the cells were obtained using a confocal microscope.

In a separate experiment, four groups of cells were cultured. The first group was treated with the DA-GQDs for 2.0 h and served as the control. The other three groups were similarly incubated with the DA-GQDs, then exposed to varying concentrations of Ca2+ solutions (2.5, 5.0, and 7.5 µM) for an additional 0.5 h. Fluorescence images of these cells were captured using a confocal microscope.

Spectroscopic investigations clearly indicated the DA decorated to GQDs. Raman spectra of the GQDs and DA-GQDs demonstrated distinct D and G bands at ∼ 1379 and 1615 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1 a). The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) was calculated to be 1.14 for the GQDs and 0.62 for the DA-GQDs. The considerable decrease in the ID/IG ratio indicates that the binding of DA to GQDs results in a reduction in the number of defect sites in the DA-GQDs. XRD patterns of the GQDs and DA-GQDs showed a distinct diffraction peak at 22.37°, attributed to the (002) crystal plane of graphene [38] (Fig. 1b). The FTIR spectra displayed prominent peaks at 1734 and 3428 cm−1 for the GQDs, corresponding to the CO stretching vibrations and –OH stretching vibrations, respectively. In the DA-GQDs, these peaks shifted from 1734 and 3428 cm−1 to 1729 and 3410 cm−1, respectively. Additional peaks at 1650 and 1136 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the amide bond and C–N vibration, were observed in the DA-GQDs (Fig. 1c). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the DA-GQDs exhibited weak absorption peaks at ∼ 230 and 280 nm, attributable to the π–π* transition of the CC bond and n–π* transition of the CO group (Fig. 1d).

Chemical composition and structural changes of the GQDs and DA-GQDs were further characterized via XPS analysis. The XPS survey spectra of the GQDs and DA-GQDs are shown in Fig. S1a. The XPS spectrum of the GQDs primarily exhibited peaks for C and O with a C/O atomic ratio of 6.53. Meanwhile, the survey spectrum of the DA-GQDs clearly showed the presence of N, in addition to the original C and O elements. As depicted in Fig. S1b, the C1s spectrum of the GQDs displays three types of C atoms, corresponding to the C–C/CC, C–OH, and COOH groups at 284.62, 285.52, and 289.52 eV, respectively. Compared to free GQDs, the C1s spectrum of the DA-GQDs in Fig. S1c showed additional peaks for C–N at 286.11 eV and N–CO at 288.91 eV. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S1d) revealed the presence of the amide bond (N–CO) at 399.50 eV. These XPS results confirmed the successful preparation of the GQDs and DA-GQDs.

TEM characterization revealed that the GQDs were spherical particles with sizes of 2–5 nm (Fig. 2 a). The DA-GQDs also exhibited the shape of spherical particles (Fig. 2b). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) results for the DA-GQDs indicated a topographic height of ∼ 4.5–5.5 nm, suggesting the presence of 1–3 graphene layers (Fig. 2c) [36,37]. The fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs and DA-GQDs under excitation at 253 nm are depicted in Fig. 2d.

The matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometer (MALDI-TOF MS) was used to further analyze the molecular structures [39,40]. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the m/z value of GQDs (M1) was recorded at 1024.48. After the loss of one –COOH group, an m/z value of 979.67 was observed. When the GQDs lost one –COOH group, one –OH group, and one CCH group, the resulting m/z value was 935.32. The m/z value of the DA-GQDs was determined to be 1159.28 (Fig. 3b). Upon removing one –OH group from the DA-GQDs, the m/z value shifted to 1142.41. The breakdown of the DA-GQD structure yielded m/z values of 1064.62 and 996.71, corresponding to the species [M2-3OH-3O + H]+ and [M2-8OH-(CCH) + H]+, respectively. A detailed explanation of the various species, including detected m/z values and calculated m/z values, is provided in Table S1. The proposed molecular configurations for the GQDs and DA-GQDs are depicted in Fig. 3c and 3d, respectively. The calculated C/O ratio of 6.8 for the proposed structure of the GQDs closely matches the value determined via the XPS analysis, as mentioned earlier.

The DA-GQDs exhibited two emission peaks at 314 and 414 nm when excited at 258 nm, the formation of two peaks attributed to the emission of DA and GQDs, respectively. As depicted in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity of DA-GQDs considerably increased at pH 4.6–5.8. For pH values ranging from 6.4 to 8.2, the fluorescence intensity decreased. As Ca2+ readily bind to hydroxyl groups in alkaline solutions, a pH of 6.0 was selected for fluorescence detection in aqueous solutions. Upon the gradual addition of Ca2+ to the DA-GQD solution, a notable redshift occurred from 314 and 414 nm to 338 and 440 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the DA-GQDs increased with the addition of varying amounts of Ca2+. To investigate the selectivity and sensitivity of the DA-GQDs toward metal ions and biomolecules, fluorescence titration experiments were conducted. The fluorescence intensity of the DA-GQDs markedly increased upon the addition of different concentrations of Ca2+ (Fig. 4 a). Other biomolecules and ions did not exhibit the same fluorescence response. A good linear correlation between the relative fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of Ca2+ was obtained in the range of 4.93–10.61 µM (Fig. 4b). The LOD was calculated to be 0.05 µM. The calibration equations were (F − F0)/F0 = 0.27 × CCa2+ − 0.89 (at E338nm) and (F − F0)/F0 = 0.059 × CCa2+ − 0.092 (at E440nm), with regression constants of 0.99 and 0.98, respectively (Fig. 4b, c). Within the Ca2+ concentration range of 3.95–9.21 μM, the ratio of fluorescence intensities (F338nm/F440nm) showed a linear relationship, described by the equation: F338nm/F440nm = 0.33 × CCa2+ + 0.25 (R2 = 0.97) (Fig. 4d). The LOD was determined to be 0.08 µM based on the fluorescence ratio. In comparison, the fluorescence intensity of free DA slightly decreased after adding Ca2+ (Fig. S3a). The performance of the DA-GQDs in detecting Ca2+ was compared with previously reported sensors in Table S2. Most of the reported sensors can determine Ca2+ at micro-molar concentrations. Although the carboxylic acid-carbon dots had the lowest LOD of 77 pM in the aqueous solution, they were not used in cell or bioorganic tissue imaging due to fluorescence quenching (See supplement materials).

Based on the MALDI-TOF mass spectrum results, the structural model of the DA-GQDs was proposed as shown in Fig. 3. Given that GQDs are derived from graphene oxide, they contain numerous oxygen atoms [41,42]. The introduced carbonyl groups enhance the electron-attracting properties of the GQDs, as documented in several studies [43,44]. To verify the mechanism of DA-GQDs sensing Ca2+, DFT calculations were conducted (Fig. 5 ). The fluorescence intensity enhancement and redshift are attributed to the inhibition of PET and the strengthening of ICT [45,46]. In the PET process, DA and GQDs act as the electron donor and acceptor, respectively. Upon excitation at 258 nm, an electron on the HOMO of GQDs transitions to its LUMO, whereas an electron from the HOMO of DA transfers to the HOMO of GQDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching. The electron transfer from DA suppresses ICT in the GQDs with a large conjugation structure. The GQDs typically function as an electron donor-π-electron acceptor (D-π-A) system. The donor functional group readily transfers its electrons to the acceptor module, resulting in the formation of positive and negative centers and creating a dipole. Under optical excitation, this electron transfer shifts the dipole, causing a shift in the fluorescence peak. As shown in Fig. 5b and 5c, the sp2 carbon rings with multiple oxygens serve as the electron acceptors, whereas the nitrogen-containing moiety acts as the electron donor. Binding with Ca2+, the interaction between Ca2+ and the DA-GQDs disrupts the electron transfer from DA to the GQDs, blocking PET and opening the ICT course, leading to fluorescence redshift and enhancement. In a typical ICT system, the positive Ca2+ interacts with the acceptor, reducing the energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO of the quantum dots (Table S3), which shifts the emission peak [47]. The fluorescence titration and DFT calculations suggest that Ca2+ predominantly binds to the oxygen on the GQD units, inducing a fluorescence redshift and increase. Also, the element distribution mapping results confirmed the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and calcium elements (Fig.S4). Subsequently, the fluorescence lifetime of DA-GQDs/Ca2+ increased from 10.4 to 19.2 ns (Fig. S3b).

However, the coordination of Ca2+ to the DA-GQDs is complicated due to the presence of multiple oxygen atoms. To simplify, three coordination modes were investigated using DFT calculations to determine the binding energies. The calcium used in the system is CaCl2, and it was placed in three typical positions: (i) near two neighboring oxygen atoms on a GQD unit; (ii) near an amide; and (iii) near a catechol moiety. The optimal geometries of the three models are shown in Fig. 5d–4f. The binding energies (Ead) of the three models are −1169.11, −2579.56, and −541.93 kcal/mol, respectively, suggesting that the configurations of Ca2+ chelating to the GQD oxygen atoms are more stable. The bond length of Ca–O (amide oxygen) is 2.213 Å, which is shorter than that of the other two models. The monodentate binding of Ca2+ to amide is also seen in coordination with N,N′-[oxybis(3-oxapentamethyleneoxy)-2-phenyl] bis(9-anthracenecarboxamide), inducing a twisted ICT signal transduction [48]. The Ca–O = C bond of the amide unit was more stable in the ethylenediamine-N,N,N′,N′-tetraacetamide complex with Ca2+ [49]. Although Fe3+ quenched the fluorescence of the DA-GQDs by chelating to the catechol of DA [50], it did not affect the coordination of Ca2+ to the GQD moiety or lead to an increase in the fluorescence signals. It is well known that Fe3+ tends to form Fe(OH)3 without interfering with the sensing of Ca2+ at pH 6.0. The coordination of Ca2+ to the oxygen atoms of GQDs in the DA-GQDs favors the selective detection of Ca2+ in aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the DA-GQDs to Ca2+
ce:section.ce:para: To further confirm the selectivity of the DA-GQDs for Ca2+, competitive experiments were performed in the presence of other ions and biomolecules, including Fe3+, Mg2+, K+, Sr2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Na+, glucose, fructose, maltose, lactose, glucosamine, and glutamate sodium (Fig. 6 ). They are typical species may interfere the binding of Ca2+. The results verified that only Ca2+ showed a notable fluorescence response, whereas other ions and biomolecules exhibited weak or no fluorescence response in the fluorescence titration experiments. The competition experiments showed that the DA-GQDs still exhibited higher fluorescence responses to Ca2+, suggesting that the Ca-induced fluorescence response was almost unaffected in the presence of five equivalents of interfering substances coexisting in the solution. Subsequently, the DA-GQDs were used to determine the Ca2+ content in organic compound calcium gluconate. After adding various concentrations of calcium gluconate, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-DA was gradually enhanced (Fig. S5a). The calibration equations were (F − F0)/F0 = 0.023 × CCa2+ − 0.098 at E338nm and (F − F0)/F0 = 0.012 × CCa2+ − 0.023 at E440nm, within the concentration range of 14.56–45.45 µM (Fig. S5b and S5c). The regression constants were 0.998 and 0.978, respectively. Moreover, the LOD was calculated to be 0.1 µM.

The cytotoxicity of the GQDs and DA-GQDs in living cells was evaluated using the CCK-8 assay. ARPE-19 cells were incubated with various concentrations of the GQDs and DA-GQDs for 24, 48, and 72 h. It was observed that there was no significant loss of cell viability even with the concentrations of the GQDs being as high as 150 µg/mL for 72 h (Fig. S6a). The cell viability of ARPE-19 incubated with the DA-GQDs remained above 70 % at a concentration of 150 µg/mL for 72 h (Fig. S6b). These results confirmed the good biocompatibility and low toxicity of the GQDs and DA-GQDs, demonstrating their potential for application in detecting intracellular Ca2+.

The capability of the DA-GQDs to image calcium fluxes was estimated using L-glutamate as an active stimulus for releasing intracellular free Ca2+ [49]. Compared to the control group (Fig. S7a–S7c), ARPE-19 cells pre-treated with L-glutamate and subsequently incubated with DA-GQDs exhibited a significance fluorescence enhancement, indicating that the DA-GQDs can effectively visualize calcium fluxes in living cells (Fig. S7d–S7f).

The practical feasibility of the DA-GQD nanosensor for detecting Ca2+ in living cells was further explored. As shown in Fig. 7 b, 7e, 7 h, and 76 k, the skeletons of the ARPE-19 cells were stained red with rhodamine phalloidin. The overlay images are depicted in Fig. 7c, 7f, 7i, and 7 l, respectively. The ARPE-19 cells incubated solely with the DA-GQDs were used as the control group (Fig. 7a–7c). Compared with the control group (Fig. 7a), a higher fluorescence signal was demonstrated in the ARPE-19 cells (Fig. 7d, 7 g, and 7j). Moreover, a progressive enhancement in fluorescence intensity was observed as the concentrations of Ca2+ gradually increased. These fluorescence imaging results confirmed that the DA-GQDs are capable of detecting Ca2+ in biological systems.

Herein, we have developed a turn-on DA-GQD fluorescence sensor for detecting Ca2+ based on the suppression of PET and the activation of ICT. The addition of Ca2+ led to coordination with the oxygen groups of the GQD units in the DA-GQDs, inhibiting the PET process and promoting the ICT course, thereby inducing fluorescence enhancement and a redshift. The theoretical molecular mechanism aligned well with the results from fluorescence titrations. This interplaying fluorescence mechanism and specific coordination mode considerably increased the selectivity and sensitivity of the DA-GQDs for Ca2+. The sensor demonstrated a robust linear detection range of 4.93–10.61 µM, with a detection limit of 0.05 µM. The results from confocal fluorescence experiments confirmed that the fluorescence sensors are cell-permeable and capable of detecting Ca2+ in living cells. More importantly, the DA-GQD nanosensor exhibited good biocompatibility and low toxicity, underscoring its remarkable biological relevance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405115, 82371042), the Wenzhou Bureau of Science and Technology (Y20210081), Project of State Key Laboratory of Ophthalmology, Optometry and Vision Science of Wenzhou Medical University (J02-20210202), “Pioneer” and “Leading Goose” R&D Program of Zhejiang (2022C03070), the Provincial Construction Project of Zhejiang (KJ-ZJ-2135), and the Huadong Medicine Joint Funds of the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LHDMY23H070004) are acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125475.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
